statusLine: EU fuel reserves are under strain with 21 of 27 countries holding critical stocks in at least one fuel type, while oil prices remain elevated at $93 per barrel.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security faces significant pressure as 21 of 27 EU countries currently hold critical reserve levels in at least one fuel category. The situation is most acute for jet fuel, where 16 countries fall below safe thresholds, followed by diesel with 15 countries in critical status. Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, and Portugal show particularly concerning profiles with critical reserves across all three fuel types.

Diesel reserves present the most widespread vulnerability, averaging just 88.8 days against the 90-day mandatory minimum. This shortfall is particularly notable given diesel's critical role in freight transport and heating. Petrol stocks average 96.3 days, providing modest cushion above requirements, while jet fuel at 87.3 days reflects ongoing aviation sector supply pressures.

The fuel supply challenges coincide with Brent crude prices holding at $93.09 per barrel, representing a 28.4% increase since late February escalations in the Middle East. This elevated price environment, combined with depleted strategic reserves released during recent supply disruptions, has created a challenging operating environment for fuel security across the bloc.
keyPoints: 21 of 27 EU countries hold critical fuel reserves in at least one category, with Luxembourg, Poland, Austria, Romania, and Portugal most severely affected across all fuel types
EU diesel reserves average 88.8 days, falling below the mandatory 90-day minimum for net imports
Jet fuel shows the broadest vulnerability with 16 countries in critical status, averaging 87.3 days of reserves
Brent crude prices remain elevated at $93.09 per barrel, up 28.4% since Middle East supply disruptions began in late February
Eastern European countries show particular strain, with Poland holding just 22.5 days of jet fuel reserves and Romania at 30.2 days for diesel
